title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: , estimate logistic regression 1000000 observations 50 covariates. vary number cores used nthreads argument parglm.control. method argument sets method used. LINPACK uses pivoted QR (dqrdc2) glm.fit final decomposition; LAPACK uses DGEQP3 LAPACK throughout; FAST solves normal equations Fisher information, similar speedglm package. plot shows median run times versus number cores. Coloured horizontal lines show single-threaded reference times glm, speedglm, glm2, fastglm, bigglm, bam. used glm.fit parglm.fit. make relative difference bigger call e.g., model.matrix model.frame take time. show point, first compute much time takes make plot. black solid line computation time model.matrix model.frame. Plot runtime versus number cores. open circles FAST method filled circles LINPACK method. FAST method, speedglm, fastglm compute Fisher information solve normal equation. advantage terms computation cost may lead unstable solutions. can alter number observations parallel chunk block_size argument parglm.control. single threaded performance parglm may slower coefficients. cause seems difference LAPACK LINPACK implementation. presumably due either QR decomposition method /qr.qty method. Windows, parglm seem slower built Rtools reason seems qr.qty method LAPACK, dormqr, slower LINPACK method, dqrsl. illustration computation times machine.
code: ##### # simulate n # number of observations #> [1] 1000000 p # number of covariates #> [1] 50 set.seed(68024947) X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p, 1/p, 1/sqrt(p)), n, ncol = p) df <- data.frame(y = 1/(1 + exp(-(rowSums(X) - 1))) > runif(n), X) ##### # compute and measure time. Setup call to make library(microbenchmark) library(speedglm) #> Loading required package: Matrix #> Loading required package: MASS #> Loading required package: biglm #> Loading required package: DBI library(fastglm) library(glm2) #>  #> Attaching package: 'glm2' #> The following object is masked from 'package:MASS': #>  #>     crabs library(biglm) library(mgcv) #> Loading required package: nlme #> This is mgcv 1.9-4. For overview type '?mgcv'. #>  #> Attaching package: 'mgcv' #> The following object is masked from 'package:fastglm': #>  #>     negbin library(parglm) X_mat <- model.matrix(y ~ ., df) biglm_form <- reformulate(names(df)[-1], response = "y") bam_form   <- biglm_form cl <- list(   quote(microbenchmark),   glm      = quote(glm     (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   speedglm = quote(speedglm(y ~ ., family = binomial(), data = df)),   glm2     = quote(glm2    (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   fastglm  = quote(fastglm (X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial())),   bigglm   = quote(bigglm  (biglm_form, df, family = binomial())),   bam      = quote(bam     (bam_form, family = binomial(), data = df)),   times = 11L) tfunc <- function(method = "LINPACK", nthreads)   parglm(y ~ ., binomial(), df, control = parglm.control(method = method,                                                          nthreads = nthreads)) cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(nthreads = .(i))))) names(cl)[9:(9L + n_threads - 1L)] <- paste0("parglm-LINPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "FAST")))) names(cl)[(9L + n_threads):(9L + 2L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-FAST-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "LAPACK")))) names(cl)[(9L + 2L * n_threads):(9L + 3L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-LAPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- as.call(cl) cl # the call we make #> microbenchmark(glm = glm(y ~ ., binomial(), df), speedglm = speedglm(y ~  #>     ., family = binomial(), data = df), glm2 = glm2(y ~ ., binomial(),  #>     df), fastglm = fastglm(X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial()),  #>     bigglm = bigglm(biglm_form, df, family = binomial()), bam = bam(bam_form,  #>         family = binomial(), data = df), times = 11L, `parglm-LINPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L), `parglm-LINPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L), `parglm-LINPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L), `parglm-LINPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L), `parglm-LINPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L), `parglm-FAST-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-LAPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"))  out <- eval(cl) #> Warning in microbenchmark(glm = glm(y ~ ., binomial(), df), speedglm = #> speedglm(y ~ : less accurate nanosecond times to avoid potential integer #> overflows s <- summary(out) # result from `microbenchmark` print(s[, c("expr", "min", "mean", "median", "max")], digits  = 3,       row.names = FALSE) #>               expr  min mean median  max #>                glm 2489 2753   2601 3523 #>           speedglm  761  910    828 1563 #>               glm2 2505 2781   2710 3247 #>            fastglm 2022 2333   2121 3726 #>             bigglm 6424 6678   6538 7732 #>                bam 5095 5465   5332 6065 #>   parglm-LINPACK-1 1864 2030   1974 2493 #>   parglm-LINPACK-2 1688 1802   1793 1926 #>   parglm-LINPACK-3 1579 1648   1635 1771 #>   parglm-LINPACK-4 1557 1663   1629 1815 #>   parglm-LINPACK-5 1477 1640   1597 1913 #>   parglm-LINPACK-6 1429 1609   1532 2289 #>   parglm-LINPACK-7 1411 1515   1492 1726 #>   parglm-LINPACK-8 1379 1558   1511 1796 #>   parglm-LINPACK-9 1407 1534   1499 1870 #>  parglm-LINPACK-10 1385 1505   1489 1684 #>      parglm-FAST-1  473  669    620 1398 #>      parglm-FAST-2  375  458    427  587 #>      parglm-FAST-3  385  491    451  800 #>      parglm-FAST-4  356  421    397  499 #>      parglm-FAST-5  358  420    402  541 #>      parglm-FAST-6  347  462    433  804 #>      parglm-FAST-7  374  443    387  570 #>      parglm-FAST-8  343  451    391  746 #>      parglm-FAST-9  356  449    411  709 #>     parglm-FAST-10  353  517    441  915 #>    parglm-LAPACK-1 1865 2082   2029 2553 #>    parglm-LAPACK-2 1631 1760   1772 1926 #>    parglm-LAPACK-3 1618 1717   1673 1992 #>    parglm-LAPACK-4 1538 1676   1695 1789 #>    parglm-LAPACK-5 1482 1694   1607 2591 #>    parglm-LAPACK-6 1420 1528   1488 1635 #>    parglm-LAPACK-7 1420 1543   1510 1737 #>    parglm-LAPACK-8 1401 1506   1469 1659 #>    parglm-LAPACK-9 1446 1569   1584 1694 #>   parglm-LAPACK-10 1383 1506   1463 1781 modmat_time <- microbenchmark(   modmat_time = {     mf <- model.frame(y ~ ., df); model.matrix(terms(mf), mf)   }, times = 10) modmat_time # time taken by `model.matrix` and `model.frame` #> Unit: milliseconds #>         expr       min        lq        mean      median         uq        max #>  modmat_time 93.452202 95.398841 129.2118116 112.9241885 163.799797 189.640826 #>  neval #>     10 par(mar = c(4.5, 4.5, .5, 9)) o <- aggregate(time ~ expr, out, median)[, 2] / 10^9 ylim <- range(o, 0); ylim[2] <- ylim[2] + .04 * diff(ylim)  o_linpack <- o[7L:(n_threads + 6L)] o_fast    <- o[(n_threads + 7L):(2L * n_threads + 6L)] o_lapack  <- o[(2L * n_threads + 7L):(3L * n_threads + 6L)]  ref_cols <- c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7") ref_lty  <- c("dashed", "dotted", "dotdash", "longdash", "twodash", "1373")  plot(1:n_threads, o_linpack, xlab = "Number of cores", yaxs = "i",      ylim = ylim, ylab = "Run time (s)", pch = 16, cex = 1.4) points(1:n_threads, o_fast,   pch = 1, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) points(1:n_threads, o_lapack, pch = 4, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) for (i in 1:6)   abline(h = o[i], lty = ref_lty[i], col = ref_cols[i], lwd = 2) abline(h = median(modmat_time$time) / 10^9, lty = "solid", col = "black",        lwd = 2) usr <- par("usr") par(xpd = TRUE) legend(x = usr[2], y = usr[4], xjust = 0, yjust = 1,        legend = c("glm", "speedglm", "glm2", "fastglm", "bigglm", "bam",                   "model.matrix", "parglm LINPACK", "parglm FAST",                   "parglm LAPACK"),        lty = c(ref_lty, "solid", NA, NA, NA),        lwd = c(2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, NA, NA, NA),        col = c(ref_cols, "black", "black", "black", "black"),        pch = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 16, 1, 4),        bty = "n") qr1 <- qr(X) qr2 <- qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE) microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   `qr LINPACK`     = qr(X),   `qr LAPACK`      = qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE),   `qr.qty LINPACK` = qr.qty(qr1, df$y),   `qr.qty LAPACK`  = qr.qty(qr2, df$y),   times = 11) #> Unit: milliseconds #>            expr        min          lq         mean     median          uq #>      qr LINPACK 421.704188 424.8740415 473.99889036 438.571424 475.7963285 #>       qr LAPACK 370.643116 374.2026335 383.83491073 378.620527 386.9735800 #>  qr.qty LINPACK  47.407275  47.9204720  59.63522682  48.196853  49.6007135 #>   qr.qty LAPACK 111.485437 112.0301015 131.62818909 112.267635 112.7435630 #>         max neval #>  670.709365    11 #>  421.638711    11 #>  169.130207    11 #>  324.167443    11

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: smaller datasets per-call overhead parglm’s thread pool can exceed gains parallelism. repeat benchmark n = 100,000 n = 10,000, dropping bam since aimed much larger problems. Note default block_size parglm.control 10000, n = 10,000 work split across threads parglm effectively runs single-threaded regardless nthreads.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 10,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: per-row cost method scales roughly \(p^2\) (computing \(X'WX\) IRLS iteration), parglm’s thread-pool overhead roughly fixed per call. two effects compete: small \(p\) overhead dominates parallel benefit, larger \(p\) parallel work amortizes overhead parglm pulls ahead. contrast n = 100,000, p = 5 (overhead wins) n = 1,000,000, p = 20 (parallel work wins).

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000 p = 5.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 1,000,000 p = 20.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: simulate Poisson dataset 20 clusters 10 observations , cluster-level random effect induces within-cluster correlation.
code: library(parglm) library(sandwich) library(lmtest) set.seed(1) n          <- 200 cluster_id <- rep(1:20, each = 10) x1         <- rnorm(n) x2         <- rnorm(n) u          <- rep(rnorm(20, sd = 0.5), each = 10)  # cluster random effect y          <- rpois(n, exp(0.5 + 0.3 * x1 - 0.2 * x2 + u)) dat        <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2, cluster = cluster_id)

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
code: fit <- parglm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = dat, family = poisson(),               control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L))

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: default model-based standard errors assume Poisson variance equals mean. small cluster random effects induce overdispersion.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: vcovHC() computes sandwich standard errors robust misspecification variance function. HC3 (default) recommended small moderate samples.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: vcovCL() accounts within-cluster correlation, appropriate correction . expected, cluster-robust standard errors larger model-based ones, reflecting extra variability due cluster random effects.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: covariance matrices also available:

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: model = TRUE (default parglm) must set model frame stored, allowing sandwich reconstruct design matrix internally.

title: Parallel GLM
text: parglm package provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS. can install release version CRAN install development version GitHub calling: See vignette example run times details.

title: parglm: Parallel GLM — parglm-package
text: Provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS.

title: parglm: Parallel GLM — parglm-package
text: Maintainer: Tom Palmer remlapmot@hotmail.com (ORCID) Authors: Tom Palmer remlapmot@hotmail.com (ORCID) Benjamin Christoffersen boennecd@gmail.com (ORCID) contributors: Anthony Williams [copyright holder] Boost developers [copyright holder]

what: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Auxiliary function parglm fitting.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: parglm.control(   epsilon = 1e-08,   maxit = 25,   trace = FALSE,   nthreads = parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L),   block_size = NULL,   method = "LINPACK",   nthreads_auto = missing(nthreads) )

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: epsilon positive convergence tolerance. maxit integer giving maximal number IWLS iterations. trace logical indicating output produced estimation. nthreads number cores use. Defaults parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L), leaves one core free. may get best performance using available physical cores data set sufficiently large. block_size number observations include parallel block. method string specifying method use. Either "LINPACK", "LAPACK", "FAST". nthreads_auto logical; internal use . Records whether nthreads auto-detected (suppresses thread-reduction warning dataset small). set argument directly.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: list components named arguments.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: LINPACK method uses QR method glm.fit final QR decomposition. dqrdc2 method described qr. QR decompositions last made DGEQP3 LAPACK. See Wood, Goude, Shaw (2015) details QR method. FAST method computes Fisher information solves normal equation. faster less numerically stable.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Wood, S.N., Goude, Y. & Shaw S. (2015) Generalized additive models large datasets. Journal Royal Statistical Society, Series C 64(1): 139-155.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: # use one core f1 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 1L))  # use two cores (mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

what: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: Function like glm can make computation parallel. function supports families listed family. See "vignette("parglm", "parglm")" run time examples.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: parglm(   formula,   family = gaussian,   data,   weights,   subset,   na.action,   start = NULL,   offset,   control = list(...),   contrasts = NULL,   model = TRUE,   x = FALSE,   y = TRUE,   ... )  parglm.fit(   x,   y,   weights = rep(1, NROW(x)),   start = NULL,   etastart = NULL,   mustart = NULL,   offset = rep(0, NROW(x)),   family = gaussian(),   control = list(),   intercept = TRUE,   ... )

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: formula object class formula. family family object. data optional data frame, list environment containing variables model. weights optional vector 'prior weights' used fitting process. NULL numeric vector. subset optional vector specifying subset observations used fitting process. na.action function indicates happen data contain NAs. start starting values parameters linear predictor. offset can used specify priori known component included linear predictor fitting. control list parameters controlling fitting process. parglm.fit passed parglm.control. contrasts optional list. See contrasts.arg model.matrix.default. model logical value indicating whether model frame included component returned value. x, y parglm: logical values indicating whether response vector model matrix used fitting process returned components returned value. parglm.fit: x design matrix dimension n * p, y vector observations length n. ... parglm: arguments used form default control argument supplied directly. parglm.fit: unused. etastart starting values linear predictor. supported. mustart starting values vector means. supported. intercept logical. intercept included null model?

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: glm object returned glm differs mainly qr element. qr element object returned parglm(.fit) \(R\) matrix QR decomposition.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: current implementation uses min(.integer(n / p), nthreads) threads n number observations, p number covariates, nthreads nthreads element list returned parglm.control. Thus, likely little () reduction computation time p almost equal n. current implementation handle p > n. Since parglm returns standard glm object, compatible sandwich package heteroskedasticity-consistent (HC) cluster-robust standard errors via vcovHC vcovCL. requires model = TRUE (default). See vignette("sandwich", "parglm") examples.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: # mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads (>= 16 rows per thread) f1 <- glm   (mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log")) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

title: parglm (development version)
text: Add biglm, fastglm, glm2, mgcv timing comparison vignette. add timings fewer observations fewer coefficients. Bump roxygen2 8.0.0 Remove busy-wait polling qr_parallel::get_stacks_res Return eta mu parallelglm skip R-side recomputation Sum upper triangle per-chunk Fisher information FAST path Drop const R_F members enable move semantics Hoist NA-zeroing beta per-chunk workers Use thread_local index thread_pool::get_id Skip intermediate matrix reverse-pivoting R Cache parglm_supported keys avoid rebuilding family objects per fit Change default parglm.control(nthreads) parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L) make better use available cores Implement persistent thread pool Replace linked-list task queue deque single mutex Fuse X chunk copy weight scaling one memory pass Support quasibinomial() quasipoisson() family specification Enable full compatibility sandwich package robust standard errors, including adding comments parglm helpfile, new vignette, tests Add fastglm’s method = 3L benchmarks parglm vignette Allow two column response family binomial quasibinomial Fix issue order summary.glm

title: parglm 0.1.8
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-21 Remove specification C++11 Amend std::result_of std::invoke_result header file Fix infinite recursion QR_base::qyt removing std::move Require C++17 Linux, macOS, Windows New maintainer - Tom Palmer

title: parglm 0.1.7
text: CRAN release: 2021-10-14 Avoid virtual function calls remove macros. Fix issue due new STRICT_R_HEADERS variable Rcpp.

title: parglm 0.1.6
text: CRAN release: 2020-08-10 Avoid memory allocations. Fix test issue ATLAS.

title: parglm 0.1.4
text: CRAN release: 2020-01-07 stops variables observations. Previously, caused crash. Handle Fortran string length argument.

title: parglm 0.1.3
text: CRAN release: 2019-03-18 Fix bug found Valgrind.

title: parglm 0.1.2
text: CRAN release: 2019-03-14 Minor changes implementation. Fix bugs patched R oldrel R.

title: parglm 0.1.1
text: CRAN release: 2019-01-19 FAST method added computes Fisher information solves normal equation speedglm. One change decreased computation time. Minor bug fixes.
